metadata.title: Mining ideological discourse on Twitter: The case of extremism in Arabic

abstract: Extremism has been a problematic term to define and suggests different and opposing meanings. This study explores how Twitter users conceptualize extremism in Arabic and express their opinions/arguments to construct the term. A corpus of tweets was collected from Twitter API using the word ‫متطرف'‬ ‫أو‬ ‫'تطرف‬ in Arabic for extremist/extremism. A topic modeling algorithm was then applied to the dataset to uncover latent associated concepts underlying extremism, followed by a critical discourse analysis using Van Dijk's Sociocognitive approach. The discursive and linguistic strategies used by Twitter users to support and justify their views of extremism were examined. The findings demonstrate an ideological influence that controls the concept of extremism, keeping it open to manipulation to serve shared interests and goals. Arab users of Twitter use extremism to promote their groups' positive schema against others-negative schema.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: Extremism has been one of the recurrent issues in which governments, organizations, and individuals in several parts of the world vary in their conception of the term apart from countering it. Part of the extremism phenomenon's complexity is that multiple factors are at interplay, that is, political, sociological, psychological, and educational. The consequences of extremism may affect our day-to-day lives, and here hate and violence are a few examples. Of course, not every extremism ends up in violence or hate; however, it is difficult in many cases to describe if an act or discourse fosters extremism and whether it may harm others. As an abstract concept, extremism varies in meaning and use among individuals and organizations due to many (e.g. sociocultural, political) differences. These differences or somewhat opposing views may be informed by how extremism was formed and introduced to the public at first. Several parties, that is, governments, institutions or organizations, social groups, and individuals, can contribute to the construction of extremism using various means such as institutional policies and media.

Individuals used to have limited access to media than governments or organizations; however, nowadays, social media have been a vibrant platform to express and contribute to nearly any issue. Social media/networks are here broadly defined as online platforms that allow users to publish and exchange content, such as Twitter, Facebook, and Youtube. The rise of social media introduced a new discourse genre that becomes part of everyday life to many and has made it easier for individuals to access a wide range of audiences across the world. The content published by users is known as User Generated Content (UGC). However, the growing use of social media is also accompanied by extremist activities to promote violence, hate, racism, or other harmful content directly or indirectly. Thus, social media face the dilemma of empowering individuals or organizations to exercise their right of expression and, at the same time, are pressed to disallow, publishing harmful content like extremism discourse. It may be useful to explicate how the concept of extremism is actualized in a real context. In other words, part of countering extremism is understanding what it means and how language users use it rather than limiting ourselves to the meaning in the dictionaries and those made by the press. Since language users or individuals are now more active on social media, it is tempting to approach the concept of extremism with emphasis on how they discursively construct it.

In this paper, I argue that the opposing views on conceptualizing extremism are attributed, in part, to competing ideologies that strive to control this concept to maintain power. Naturally, efforts to identify and respond to extremism should include how people view or think about it. This research is not concerned with countering extremism but rather examining the discourse of extremism in Arabic and its representations on Twitter to identify ideological underpinnings that force the concept to be open to manipulation. Here, I start with determining topics characteristic of extremism discourse, followed by analyzing the discourse structures and discursive strategies.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The Online Etymology Dictionary (2019) defines extremism as the 'disposition to go to extremes in doctrine or practice'. The concept of extremism is closely related to and sometimes is interchangeably used with radicalization. According to a report by the European Union (Alonso et al., 2008), radicalization is considered as socialization to extremism. The commission of the European Union views violent extremism as a consequence of radicalization (European Commission, 2016). There are mainly two views on the overlap of radicalization and extremism: the first of which emphasizes extremism as informed by an ideological background that seeks to overthrow a regime (Joffé, 2013). The other emphasizes extremism as a form of exerting native ideologies (Pisoiu, 2012). It is no surprise that ideology is a distinguishing factor between radicalization and extremism, for it is a driving force to effect a desired change. Extremism is sometimes distinguished based on violence as in violent extremism and non-violent extremism to give the impression of whether it is acceptable. UNESCO has conducted several initiatives and reports (e.g. intergovernmental programs, conferences) to respond to extremism and has concluded that academic research that constitutes our understanding of violent extremism and the role of the internet and social media is still lacking (Alava et al., 2017). It can be noticed from the studies mentioned above that there is a fine line between violent and non-violent extremism discourse, which allows it to spread over social media without literally promoting violence or hate, making the concept fuzzy.

The literature on the concept of extremism in Arabic does not provide a clear definition of extremism, which urges researchers to apply the general meaning to specific areas of study (e.g. politics, religion, education). Classical Arabic dictionaries such as al Qamuus 1 al Muheet and Lisan al Arab either did not include entries on extremism or used a general sense as the case with al Mu'djam al Waseet referring extremism to one exceeding the limit of moderation and not taking a moderate position. In contemporary dictionaries such as Almaany, al Rae'd, and al Ghany, extremism is going to an extreme as in going to the north or when the sun sets (going to the extreme west). Extremism is also used in these dictionaries to refer to political, religious, sectarian, and intellectual fanaticism, a dangerous and destructive approach to individuals and groups.

Some scholars tried to define extremism and make this phenomenon possible for research. According to Almubarak (2006: 21), extremism is 'deviation in understanding a doctrine, belief, philosophy, thought and holding an excessive prejudice to a particular understanding. . .. ' Bayumi (1999), on the other hand, views extremism as a movement toward the social and moral base though going beyond the limits set by society. Naggar (2003) emphasizes the negative connotation of the term and describes extremism as an aggressive attitude toward an existing social system intended to impose a radical change. Other attempts to define extremism introduced similar views (see also Abdullah and Muhammad, 2020;Ahmad and Hussain, 2020;Shahata and Annaggar, 2003;Zakeer, 2016).

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: Identifying extremism has been an open problem since it is hard to distinguish violent from the non-violent extremist discourse. Some research attempts, though limited, examined the discourses on and surrounding extremism. For instance, Smith et al. (2019) studied the construction of extremism in the U.S. media over 25 years and found that extremism has been framed as a security issue. The inappropriate use of terms such as terrorism and fundamentalism interchangeably with extremism in the press is also a significant factor in misunderstanding extremism (John Collins et al., 2002;Smith, 2007). Other researchers studied the discourse of what they claimed as extremist groups online using corpus linguistics techniques (see Brindle, 2009;Prentice et al., 2012) and demonstrated their associated topics (e.g. racism, morality) and the ideologies behind them. However, these studies did not make it clear how extremist groups and their discourse were determined. Another strand of research developed technical solutions to capture extremist content on social media using algorithms and metrics (see Benigni et al., 2017;Berger and Morgan, 2015;Bhattacharjee et al., 2017;Chatfield et al., 2015;Sureka and Agarwal, 2014;Wei et al., 2016). However, technically oriented research may not be enough to discern extremist discourse due to the implicit meaning of such discourse and the different languages used, which might not be supported.

The absence of a clear-cut definition of extremism to capture the extremist content on social media has further complicated researchers' efforts. The complexity is partly due to the relative view or judgment on what is to be considered extremism. In other words, what is thought of as extremism varies among individuals or social groups and may change over time. However, it is generally accepted that extremism is departing to either end of the moderation continuum.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: Twitter has been one of the most popular microblogging services in the world. According to Statista (2019) report, the average number of monthly active users is 330 million posting about 500 million tweets a day as estimated by Internet Live Stats (2019). In 2021, two Arab countries (Saudi Arabia with over 12 million users and Egypt having more than 4 million users) were among the world top 20 in terms of the number of twitter users (Statista, 2021). Twitter allows users to publish and share diverse content as tweets like short text, images, videos, and links. Users can interact with other users in several ways, that is, chatting and sharing content and follow their updates, among other available functionalities (Murthy, 2018). In addition to posting and connecting with other users, Twitter's most distinctive feature is encouraging short posts (limited to 280 characters), which lowers the time and thought of communication (Java et al., 2007). Twitter is also a source of real-time news and information. Users can create and participate in a Hashtag (a topic open for users to post) in which Twitter brings the discussion/posts together.

Twitter has been a virtual space for individuals to contribute to or comment on a wide range of sociopolitical issues taking some power from traditional media. Enhanced by the ease of use and access to the public, Twitter has become an increasingly powerful medium to activism and political movements such as Occupy Wall Street against economic inequality and the 2011 call for street protests in Uganda against fuel prices (Moore, 2011). Controversies and conflicts over any topic or event may turn into a heated political debate on Twitter at the national and international levels. The 2011 so-called 'Arab Spring' is one typical example of microblogging platforms' role to serve users' shared goals, in that individuals exploited Twitter to organize protests and comment on the political situation (Warf, 2011).

Concerns about misusing the service to promote unwanted content like extremism are frequently expressed. However, the microblogging service reiterates that fighting violent extremism has been part of its effort to provide a safe service, as stated in The Twitter Rules (2019). The 12th biannual Transparency Report of Twitter (2018) revealed that they shut down over 1 million accounts since 2015 due to the promotion of terrorist content.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: Like other critical issues in several parts of the world, such as racism and immigration, extremism evokes different and opposing views among Arabs, mainly about using the term to describe an act or thought. Opposing views are likely to reflect on language use where individuals express whether their support on social issues. Here, critical discourse studies (CDS) emerge as a theory and analytical tool to study language as a social practice (see Chouliaraki and Fairclough, 1999;Gee, 2004;Van Dijk, 1993). It is concerned with sociopolitics where power relations and inequality are central to CDS and ideology. Individuals are members of social groups who share values, attitudes, and ideologies and seek to control their commonalities by enacting them in their discourse. CDS are believed to help unfold discursive strategies characteristic of prejudiced use of language.

Several approaches have been proposed within CDS, such as the Dialectical-Relational Approach (Fairclough, 1992(Fairclough, , 1995)), the Sociocognitive Approach (Van Dijk, 1995a, 1995b, 2015, 2016), and the Discourse-Historical Approach (Reisigl and Wodak, 2001;Wodak, 2001). These approaches are qualitative; however, corpus-based methods have been used widely to study discourse showing meaningful findings (Caldas-Coulthard and Moon, 2010;Partington, 2003). They apply quantitative and qualitative methods to use large collections of texts to find out interesting patterns. According to Fairclough and Wodak (1997), research in CDS is characterized by certain principles such as addressing social problems, mediation, and viewing discourse as a form of social action. Typical to CDS is the role of ideology in that discourse and ideology are intertwined and inevitable to any approach since ideology cannot be 'read off' (Fairclough, 1992: 89). It is then of paramount importance to refer to language users' ideological background when studying the representations of critical social issues in their discourse.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The increasing popularity of Twitter results in a vast amount of data published online. Although qualitative methods are typical to CDS, it is urging to use quantitative methods in search of a better understanding of the data. This study approaches Twitter as a corpus and seeks to find out meaningful patterns of language use. Thus, the analytical framework comprises two methods: starting with topic modeling to generate quantitative data followed by critical explanation and interpretation using Van Dijk's sociocognitive approach.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: Topic modeling offers a useful way to extract information from textual materials. It was developed as a statistical method to deal with large collections of texts by identifying and grouping their words co-occurrences to form topics, which are clusters of words/terms that are more likely to appear about a particular subject (Mohr and Bogdanov, 2013;Roberts et al., 2014). Each topic produces a set of probable terms to assign to a collection of texts or documents. However, the co-occurrence of words does not have to be within limited spans but rather across the whole collection of texts. Topic modeling is informed by the concept of the bag of words in which the text is represented by word occurrences regardless of their structural aspects. Although topic modeling is a relatively new method for CDS, example studies that utilized it are (Törnberg andTörnberg, 2016a, 2016b;Vaara et al., 2019). The use of topic models in this work is due to the need to generate statistically significant words/concepts representing the underlying subjects of tweets about extremism as a first step before subsequent qualitative interpretation.

There are several approaches to apply topic modeling, such as latent Dirichlet allocation, latent semantic analysis, and non-negative matrix factorization (NMF). In this study, NMF (Lee and Seung, 1999) was used to represent topic models of extremism in Arabic. NMF is an algorithm in which a matrix of the input data is factorized into two matrices: one represents words while the other represents documents (instances of textual data). The resulting matrices contain words and documents along with their weighted frequencies of occurrences. Only non-negative elements (elements whose values are 0 and above) are considered. Each document is a distribution of topics or rather a weighted sum of topics. NMF was chosen because it was developed and found to perform better with small corpora than other approaches, according to Sarkar (2016: 246).

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The Sociocognitive Approach (SCA) is a triangulation of Discourse-Cognition-Society in which discourse structures and social structures can influence each other through a cognitive interface (Van Dijk, 2014, 2015, 2016). The discourse structure comprises several substructures, for example, phonological, syntactic, semantic, and graphical ones. The cognitive interface is represented by mental models: subjective representations of personal experiences that may overlap with socially shared knowledge, attitudes, and ideologies characteristic of a group or organization. These models are of two types: event/ situational models, which define what the discourse is about and context models, which define the communicative situation including time and setting. The social structure includes the groups (e.g. political parties, social movements) and organizations/institutions (educational, cultural, business) that try to control power and public discourse.

According to the SCA, ideology is 'The basic frameworks for organizing the social cognitions shared by members of social groups, organizations or institutions' (Van Dijk, 1995a: 17-18). It is viewed at the interface between discourse and society. Ideology controls mental models and attitudes, resulting in ideological discourse. It is crucial here to note that ideology is neither true nor false nor good nor bad in the SCA. Mental models of events and context, influenced by ideologies, reproduce biased interpretations of experiences and particular subjects besides the setting and participants. Likewise, group attitudes may be negative or biased due to ideological control. Ideological discourse is polarized and features mainly positive self-presentation and negative other description employing a range of discursive strategies. Within SCA, discourse structures are examined to find out ideological representations, often implicit. This study focused on the semantic microstructures such as proposition, presupposition, denomination, modality, and agency. Since much contextual information about tweets was insufficient for analysis, reference was limited to the event model.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: Data were collected from Twitter using Twitter Application Programming Interface (API) as a retrieval tool. The search was conducted using the two Arabic keywords ‫'تطرف'‬ and ‫'متطرف'‬ for extremism and extremist to retrieve tweets that included these words rather than using a lemma, which did not work due to some restrictions by API. The search query was conducted several times over a month between December 2019 and January 2020 and returned a random sample of about 10,000 tweets. The tweets, along with metadata, were saved in a spreadsheet.

Preprocessing data was executed using Python and associated packages including Natural Language Processing Tools (NLTK; Bird et al., 2009), Pandas, and Sklearn. The preprocessing of data, typical to text mining projects, aimed to reduce the corpus to a smaller and meaningful size and prepare them for applying the NMF algorithm. It consisted of five stages: tokenization in which words were put into units removing punctuations and abbreviations, stop words removal where high frequency/function words were removed, stemming and here prefixes and suffixes were removed from tokenized words, and lemmatization where inflected word forms were reduced to their roots available in dictionaries. Finally, the resulting preprocessed data were normalized via term frequency-inversed document frequency (TFIDF). The frequency of word and document occurrence was statistically weighted to reflect the terms/words' distribution over the documents.

Tokenization was done through NLTK using WordPunctTokenizer. A list of 750 Arabic stop words prepared by Alrefaie (2017) was used to remove high frequency words. Arabic stemmer ARLSTem (Abainia et al., 2017) was used for stemming while lemmatization was done via Farasa (Abdelali et al., 2016). Tfidfvectorizer was applied to the data to generate a matrix of TFIDF features. After the data have been preprocessed, the NMF algorithm was applied using adapted codes from Pedregosa et al. (2011) with minor changes concerning the sample size and number of topics.

paragraphs: Four topics 2 were generated after applying the NMF algorithm and each of which included ten terms. While each topic returned a set of different terms, few words appeared in more than a topic such as state/country, Islam, and ISIS. It can be noticed that topics 1 and 2 show a reasonable distribution of weight, unlike topics 3 and 4, which were distinguished by the words thought and feminine consecutively. The variation in weight distribution between the four topics may be partly referred to the co-occurrence distance between terms and what can be described as 'seasonal co-occurrence' of some terms due to trending topics at a certain time. The result may be an outlier as in topic 3 and 4. Figure 1 below shows the four topics and the distributions of weighted terms.

Topic 2 has a more significant weighted sum of terms than others along with reasonable distribution of values which can be good indicators of a higher quality. Topic 2 theme can be characterized by conflicting or rather competing terms such as state-organization, future-ignorance describing mainly ISIS organization as an example of extremism. Figure 2 demonstrates the topic size by weight and the five probable terms of each topic. sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: In [1], the situation model is Egypt's relationship, as a leading Arab country, with Israel.

[1] includes the key word country and other words with negative meanings such as problem, hate, and enemy. Denomination is used to refer to some groups as different and opponents to majority Egyptians 'I want to say that any Egyptian/Arab/Muslim. . ..'

The tweet starts with the political pronoun we in 'We as Egyptians. . .' to identify with Egyptians as a nation and promote a nationalist discourse that emphasizes an independent political approach from other Arab countries. This is further supported by recommending other (Arab) countries to work on their problems with Israel. The user assumes she expresses the majority Egyptians. She addresses Egyptians and non-Egyptians by asking a question and answering it by negating any problem between Egypt and Israel. Such negation may be taken against a dominant ideology in the Arab world that views Israel as an enemy and occupying force. This is made more apparent when the user attributes hate to Israel in the Arab world to Judaism, which implies that the Egyptian-Israeli relationship is not based on religious ideology.

The user ends this tweet by asserting that extremism, as represented by some Islamic groups, is a common enemy to Egypt and Arab countries. This assertion works as a mitigation strategy in that it suggests the relationship with Israel is not harmful or dangerous compared to the threat of extremist Islamic groups. The relationship with Israel is characterized by a negative attitude in the Arab world. Thus, a positive tone toward Israel may contradict such a shared attitude and evoke a resistant ideology, and it seems the user anticipated this reaction to her tweet in [1].

[1] ‫بنفسهم،عايزة‬ ‫يحلوها‬ ‫معاها‬ ‫مشكلة‬ ‫عندها‬ ‫تانيه‬ ‫دولة‬ ‫أي‬ ‫مفيش،‬ ‫ال‬ ‫اإلجابة‬ ‫؟‬ ‫اسرائيل‬ ‫مع‬ ‫مشكلة‬ ‫عندنا‬ ‫هل‬ ‫كمصريين‬ ‫احنا‬ ‫العربية‬ ‫والدول‬ ‫لمصر‬ ‫األكبر‬ ‫اكتر،العدو‬ ‫مش‬ ‫اليهودية‬ ‫الديانة‬ ‫بسبب‬ ‫بيبقي‬ ‫اسرائيل‬ ‫كاره‬ ‫/مسلم‬ ‫مصري/عربي‬ ‫أي‬ ‫أن‬ ‫أقول‬ ‫اسمائها‬ ‫بمختلف‬ ‫االسالميه‬ ‫والجماعات‬ ‫التطرف‬ ‫هي‬ [We as Egyptians, do we have a problem with Israel? The answer is no. Any other country that has a problem with it (Israel) should solve it themselves. I want to say that any Egyptian/Arab/Muslim, who hates Israel, should be due to no more than Judaism. The big enemy to Egypt and Arab countries is Extremism and Islamic groups under different names.]

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The situation model in [2] is the religious and political approach of the Iranian regime and Muslim brothers group who is claimed to employ Islam for political purposes. In [2],

organization is the key term used with other negative words like criminology, terrorism, destabilization, and kharijites. Agency is used here to show active responsibility for negative actions, for example, criminality, terrorism.

The user is making an analogy between the Iranian regime and the Muslim brothers group as having similar thoughts and organization though they belong to different beliefs. The Iranian regime and Muslim brothers are represented here as two groups that are different from the mainstream Muslims in that they are associated with violent acts, for example, crimes and terrorism. The ideological structure that characterizes [2] is negative other-description through the semantic strategy of disclaimer. Using a disclaimer, the user makes an apparent denial in '. . .Sunni Muslims have nothing do with that' which contributes to the avoidance of negative impression about Sunni Muslims, in one hand, and negative other (Iranian regime and Muslim brothers) presentation on the other hand.

The Iranian regime and Muslim brothers' negative representation can be referred to their shared political ideology that leads to an extremist mindset. It is implied that this mindset makes a minority in the Muslim world and works against the majority.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: In [3], the situation model is to argue about the major problems that hinder making progress in the user's community, most probably Iraq. The key term in [3] is ignorance accompanied by negative words like demagogues, criminal, ignorant, problems, and terrorism. This example tweet makes use of agency to project individuals' '. . .illiterates, demagogues, criminal and ignorant people' responsibility for negative action. Also, the modality of necessity is used in '. . .but the demagogic group will never disappear' to express inherent negative attributes.

The user starts his tweet in [3] by introducing specific dangerous individuals (e.g. illiterates, demagogues) as a universal phenomenon and this strategy is used to justify their unwanted existence. This negative description serves indirect ideological polarization in which the first sentence identifies dangerous individuals while the next is initiated by 'our' and lists the resulting problems as consequences. The indirect ideological polarization goal in [3] is to hold the dangerous individuals previously mentioned responsible for the current critical situation. Thus, the user utilizes disclaimer through blame transfer strategy, which implies that serious problems are to be blamed to the demagogic group. This may be clearer in '. . .but the demagogic group will never disappear'. Where the demagogic group might also be used as an umbrella term for the illiterates, demagogues, criminal, and ignorant people. Here we are in front of a perceived divide based on an ideological conflict between social groups. [There are illiterates, demagogues, criminals, and ignorant people in every community. Our problems are: not running the country by law, not draining off terrorism channels, and the increasing poverty rate. When (all of the problems) treated, you will see progress at all levels, but the demagogic group will never disappear.]

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The situation model here suggests introducing the United Arab Emirates (UAE) political approach to face serious problems in the area, The Middle East. Example [4] is characterized by the key term future and negative words including mistakes, oppression, and darkness. It involves a presupposition of past and present mistakes that need to be corrected. The level of description is made general in which mistakes and the parties behind them are assumed to be known. These parties are assumed to be extremist Islamic groups and the media that support them. The user starts by reaffirming his country approach and continuous efforts to deal with the mistakes using the pronoun we. He defines what he argues to be his country's typical activity in the area, correcting mistakes. Also, he emphasizes what they, UAE people, should keep doing to maintain their homeland's future, which implies a call for supporting the government approach to defend or protect 'our country' the nation against extremism. It is normal for ideological groups to focus attention on typical activities that bring them together as in [4].

The user employs 'human brotherhood' as an approach that expresses tolerance and, at the same time, communicates the norms and values his group strives for regardless of the media's negative role. Media is viewed here as having an opposing ideology against national goals. However, shared activities, norms, and values are promoted in [4] to control the outgroup mistakes. [We continue what we see necessary for our country's future and we will continue correcting past and present mistakes associated with this area and people. Nothing will stop us from doing so, no matter if extremism continues its oppression and darkness. And no matter if the media campaign against our country got intense. We chose 'human brotherhood' as an approach.]

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The situation model in [5] is to justify a perceived misconception that may lead to viewing a high commitment to religion as a form of extremism. This tweet does not include negative meanings except the search word extremism.

Example [5] starts with self-presentation using the pronoun we to draw attention to the group's attitude to others and enhance credibility. Self-presentation aims to express a positive self-schema against a potential misconception that may associate religious commitment and being responsible with extremism, which is negated. Positive self-schema is used in [5] to justify a position. This is corroborated by the counterargument rejection strategy in (And this does not mean. . .) that may suppose such attitude adopts the government approach.

Another ideological structure that contributes to self-presentation is that of interests in which ideological group members defend their shared interests. Religion is a fundamental aspect of religious groups and can be viewed as a source of power that must be maintained. Thus, any claims or negative association with religion may urge group members to refute them and respond somehow in a reactive tone as in [5].

[We are responsible people, not extremists. Religion is responsibility in front of Allah, us, and slaves. Religion is a commitment to principles and texts that do not change by time and place. Islam is the only religion that recognizes other religions and, if that agrees with the government approach, thanks to Allah. And this does not mean to go with the mainstream, dear.]

Self-presentation aims to express a positive self-schema against a potential misconception that may associate religious commitment and being responsible with extremism, which is negated. Positive self-schema is used in [5] to justify a position. This is corroborated by the counterargument rejection strategy in (And this does not mean. . .) that may suppose such attitude adopts the government approach.

Another ideological structure that contributes to self-presentation is that of interests in which ideological group members defend their shared interests. Religion is a fundamental aspect of religious groups and can be viewed as a source of power that must be maintained. Thus, any claims or negative association with religion may urge group members to refute them and respond somehow in a reactive tone as in [5].

[5]

[We are responsible people, not extremists. Religion is responsibility in front of Allah, us, and slaves. Religion is a commitment to principles and texts that do not change by time and place. Islam is the only religion that recognizes other religions and, if that agrees with the government approach, thanks to Allah. And this does not mean to go with the mainstream, dear.]

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
sections.header_raw: The construction of extremism and ideological control
sections.header_norm: the construction of extremism and ideological control
paragraphs: The concept of extremism as used by Twitter users in Arabic features topics that draw on competing ideologies. For instance, in [1] and [3], extremism is associated with dangerous minority (religious, demagogic) groups that work against governments. Conversely, some users reject to associate religious commitment with extremism as in [5] and argue that religious co-existence is plausible. While extremism is associated with minority groups at the state level in [1] and [3], it is also extended to involve regional forces with opposing ideologies like the Iranian regime and Muslim brothers as stated in [2] and alluded to in [4].

The shared sociocognitive background of Arab users contributes to the construction of extremism discourse. The relationship with the west, sectarianism, and religious intolerance are typical critical issues in the Arab world. Mental models of events/actions about these issues and others have formed over time influenced by recurring experiences and discourse. Twitter Arab users seem to have construed their accumulated personal knowledge and, thus, interpretations of such events in favor of the discourse they adopt. Although all or most members of epistemic communities may share social knowledge, some social groups may develop different attitudes and ideologies (Van Dijk, 2015). For instance, the user tries to justify having a relationship with Israel in [1] in response to a potentially negative or cautious attitude by specific groups. These negative and opposing attitudes are most probably due to different ideologies of small groups or members of the same community, which is normal. Thus, it becomes hard to tell if a social group ideology about extremism is good or bad, true or false, positive or negative, which is consistent with Van Dijk's view of ideology as an evaluative construct that is subject to specific groups or members judgment (Van Dijk, 2011).

Twitter users are social members who identify with social groups and belief systems. Influenced by their group membership and belief system, they may seek to influence others by sharing and expressing their opinions and ideas. Regardless of the motivations and credibility of content, Twitter users interact with interesting things and agree with other users who share similar views (Yardi and Boyd, 2010). Naturally, social and political discourses include issues that trigger users' reactions urging them to express themselves, and extremism is a good topic for debate. Doing so, they exercise their rights as social members without being an elite or having permission, as in traditional media.

The ideological discourse structures of extremism explained above are tailored to promote native ideology and reject opposing ones, which agrees with Pisoiu (2012). Also, by promoting native ideology, users or ingroup members may seek to recruit new members and expand group size. The construction of extremism, then, is ideologically controlled consciously or unconsciously for group interests and goals. The discourse structures of extremism and the discursive strategies, for example, disclaimer, counterargument-rejection revolve around 'ideological square' (Van Dijk, 2011), which demonstrate an emphasis on positive self-presentation and negative other-description on the one hand, and mitigating own negative properties and other positive ones, on the other hand. Thus, the debate on defining extremism seems bound to the complexity of determining where it starts and ends (Alkhawadja, 2007). This is reflected in using extremism in favor of group interests and goals, which are ideologically loaded. Almubarak (2006) views extremism from a broader perspective and argues convincingly that it is not pertinent to specific nations, religions, and culture as much as it is a universal phenomenon appropriated to serve a (political, social, religious) dogma.

Subjecting the concept of extremism to personal/group evaluation may result in manipulating the public discourse. Unfortunately, other illegitimate forms of discourse about hate, religious intolerance, and racism are motivated by extremist ideologies and feed on the concept's blurredness. Of course, some other issues and phenomena are hard to describe; however, extremism may be misused and lead to violence. Although violent and non-violent extremism are sometimes differentiated, Schmid (2014) states that the distinction between them is false and illusionary.

sections.paper_id: Hamdi - 2022 - Mining ideological discourse on Twitter The case of extremism in Arabic
paragraphs: The concept of extremism has been difficult to define, allowing individuals and social groups to adapt it to their purposes. Machine learning techniques using NMF and Van Dijk's SCA helped identify topics that characterize extremism discourse among Arab Twitter users. The underlying ideologies that forced extremism to be an evaluative construct were explored. Motivated by their group interests and goals, Arab Twitter users associated extremism with critical issues and topics like state, Islam, and organization. The construction of extremism in such issues was influenced by ideologies in which users promoted their native ideology and resisted opposing ones. The discourse structure of extremism included categories such as identification and ideological polarization. The recurring experience of events or situations that describe extremism created mental models through which users understand and interpret events. Thus, extremism is likely to continue to be subject to manipulation because it is under ideological control. An open research direction to minimize ideological control that needs to be further studied may be group members' self-awareness of shared ideologies and mental models.
